cards.title: Email Newsletters & Campaigns
cards.description: How do you stay in touch with supporters and donors? A tool like Mailchimp, or someone sending updates from their personal Gmail?
cards.why: Email is still the most reliable way to reach people who care about your mission. No newsletter means you're invisible between galas.

cards.title: Social Media Management
cards.description: Which platforms are you on, and who's posting? Is there a plan, or does someone throw something up when they remember?
cards.why: A dormant presence makes an active org look inactive. Better to do one platform well than five poorly.

cards.title: Website Content & Updates
cards.description: When's the last time someone updated your website? Are your staff bios accurate, or is there a smiling face from three EDs ago?
cards.why: Your website is the first impression for donors and the people you serve. Outdated content erodes trust fast.

cards.title: Blog / Storytelling Content
cards.description: Does your org publish impact stories, client spotlights, or program updates? Who writes them? Where do they live?
cards.why: Funders want to see impact, not just hear about it. Without stories, you're scrambling every time someone asks "what have you been up to?"

cards.title: Graphic Design & Visual Assets
cards.description: Who makes your flyers, social graphics, and event posters? Do you have a consistent logo and style, or does every piece look like a different org?
cards.why: Visual consistency builds recognition and trust. You don't need a designer — you need shared templates anyone can use.

cards.title: Email & Calendar Systems
cards.description: Does everyone have an org email address, or are people using personal accounts? Is there a shared calendar for events and deadlines?
cards.why: Org email is your identity. If a staff member leaves and their personal Gmail was the main contact, you lose access to everything connected to it.

cards.title: File Storage & Documents
cards.description: How does your org store and share documents? Google Drive, Dropbox, a shared folder on someone's laptop, or a file cabinet in the back office?
cards.why: When a key staff member leaves, can anyone find the files? Clear storage keeps you from losing — or leaking — institutional knowledge.

cards.title: Donor / CRM Database
cards.description: Where do you track donors, volunteers, and clients? A purpose-built CRM, a spreadsheet someone started in 2017, or a mix of both?
cards.why: If donor history lives in one person's head, you can't follow up effectively. When that person leaves, the relationships leave too.

cards.title: Accounting & Financial Software
cards.description: How do you manage your books? Who has access? Can your board treasurer pull a report without calling someone first?
cards.why: Clean financials are required for grants, audits, and your tax-exempt status. If only one person understands the system, you're one resignation from a crisis.

cards.title: Project & Task Management
cards.description: How does your team track who's doing what? A tool like Asana, a whiteboard, a chain of emails, or just... vibes?
cards.why: Small teams move fast, but things fall through the cracks when nothing is written down. You need a shared sense of what's in progress and what's done.

cards.title: User Onboarding & Offboarding
cards.description: When someone new starts, how do they get email, file access, and logins? When someone leaves, how do you cut access?
cards.why: Offboarding is where the real risk lives. A former employee with access to your donor database or bank account is a liability you might not discover until it's too late.

cards.title: Account Security & Passwords
cards.description: Is everyone reusing passwords? Does anyone use two-factor authentication? Is the org's Facebook password floating around in a text thread?
cards.why: One compromised password can take down your email, donor data, or bank account. A password manager and 2FA are low-cost, high-impact protections.

cards.title: Staff/Volunteer Device Management
cards.description: What devices do people use for org work — org-owned laptops, personal phones, a shared desktop? Do you know what's connecting to your systems?
cards.why: If a volunteer's laptop gets stolen with client data on it, that's your problem. Knowing what devices touch your data is step one in protecting it.

cards.title: Software Licensing & Subscriptions
cards.description: How many subscriptions is your org paying for? Who signed up for them? Are there tools nobody uses, or tools people need but can't afford?
cards.why: Subscription creep is real — $15/month here, $30 there, and suddenly you're spending thousands on software nobody's audited. Many tools offer nonprofit discounts.

cards.title: Domain Names & DNS
cards.description: Who registered your domain? Who pays to renew it? Is it under the org's account, or tied to a former board member's GoDaddy login?
cards.why: If your domain expires, your website and email vanish overnight. Boring to get right — until it's an emergency.

cards.title: Website Hosting & Reliability
cards.description: Where does your website actually live — Squarespace, WordPress, a server someone set up years ago? Who do you call when it goes down?
cards.why: Your site going down during a fundraising campaign is a missed opportunity you can't get back. Know who hosts it, what you pay, and how to get help.

cards.title: Backup & Disaster Recovery
cards.description: If your office flooded or your Google account got hacked, what would you lose? Do you have backups? Have you ever tested restoring one?
cards.why: Ransomware, accidental deletion, a failed hard drive — any of these can wipe out years of data. A backup you've never tested is barely better than none.

cards.title: Data Privacy & Compliance
cards.description: What sensitive data do you collect — client records, health info, donor financials, data from minors? Where is it stored, and who can access it?
cards.why: You may have legal obligations under HIPAA, FERPA, or state privacy laws. A data breach destroys the trust you've spent years building.

cards.title: Network & Office IT
cards.description: Who manages the Wi-Fi, the printer, the phone system? Is there a closet with a blinking router nobody's touched in three years?
cards.why: When the internet goes down before a big mailing, someone has to fix it. If nobody knows the Wi-Fi password, a simple problem becomes a lost afternoon.

cards.title: Physical Security & Surveillance
cards.description: Does your org have cameras, alarms, keycard access, or a visitor sign-in? Who monitors them? Where does the footage go?
cards.why: If clients, children, or vulnerable populations visit your space, physical security is part of your duty of care. Someone needs to own the data.

cards.title: Vendor Selection & Tech Planning
cards.description: When you need a new tool, how do you choose? Does someone Google around, or is there a process for evaluating options and checking nonprofit pricing?
cards.why: Bad tool choices compound. A CRM that doesn't talk to your email platform is hard to undo. A little planning upfront saves a lot of pain.

cards.title: Training & Digital Literacy
cards.description: How comfortable is your team with the tech they use? When you roll out a new tool, is there a plan for teaching people, or is it an afterthought?
cards.why: The best tools don't help if people can't or won't use them. Training should be part of every rollout, not something you figure out later.

cards.title: Accessibility & Inclusion
cards.description: How's your website look on a cracked Android phone with slow data? Does your newsletter work for a family that speaks Spanish or Vietnamese?
cards.why: The communities we serve often have the oldest devices and the most language barriers. Our tech should meet people where they are.

cards.title: Data & Impact Reporting
cards.description: Can you pull the numbers a funder asks for without a week of digging? Or is your data scattered across intake forms, spreadsheets, and someone's memory?
cards.why: Funders want outcomes, boards want dashboards, grant apps want specifics. If you can't answer "how many did you serve," you're leaving money on the table.

cards.title: AI & Automation
cards.description: Is your org experimenting with AI — for grant drafts, meeting notes, intake data? Or are staff quietly using ChatGPT with no org-wide conversation?
cards.why: AI is moving fast. Using it to draft a newsletter is different from using it to make decisions about clients. A deliberate conversation now beats discovering problems later.

cards.title: Who's Got This?
cards.description: Spread out all the cards. For each one, name the person responsible. Write their name on a sticky note and place it on the card. No name? That's a gap. The same name on a dozen cards? That person is stretched thin.
cards.bestFor: Team meetings, board discussions, new ED onboarding

cards.title: My Hand
cards.description: Sort every card into three piles: "I can handle this" / "I want to learn this" / "I'd rather someone else handle this." Be honest — there are no wrong answers. See your skills clearly and identify where you need support.
cards.bestFor: Solo EDs, individual contributors, anyone wearing too many hats

cards.title: Org Health Check
cards.description: Rate each card: "We're solid" / "Getting by" / "Struggling" / "Haven't started." Lay them in four columns. The last two columns are your priority list.
cards.bestFor: Annual planning, strategic retreats, grant writing prep

cards.title: Hackathon Scouting
cards.description: For each card, ask: could a team of volunteers solve this in 48 hours? Sort into "yes," "maybe," and "not a fit." For the "yes" pile, describe the problem in one sentence — that's a dsmHack project seed.
cards.bestFor: Pre-hackathon prep, dsmHack Nonprofit Tech Plan Workshop

cards.title: Budget Reality
cards.description: Sort cards into four piles: "Free / internal" / "We pay for this" / "We should be paying but aren't" / "Paying too much." Pile three is where risk lives. Pile four is where savings hide.
cards.bestFor: Budget season, finance committee meetings, cost audits
